name: GPU Architecture Fundamentals
description: Core GPU architecture concepts including CUDA cores, Tensor Cores, and memory hierarchy
key_facts: NVIDIA GPUs use Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs) each containing CUDA cores for parallel computation
Tensor Cores are specialized hardware units that accelerate matrix multiply-and-accumulate operations for AI workloads
GPU memory hierarchy includes registers, shared memory, L1/L2 cache, and global HBM (High Bandwidth Memory)
The NVIDIA Hopper architecture (H100) introduced the Transformer Engine for mixed-precision training
PCIe and NVLink are the two primary interconnects for GPU-to-host and GPU-to-GPU communication
common_misconceptions: More CUDA cores always means better AI performance (Tensor Cores and memory bandwidth are often the bottleneck for AI workloads)
All GPU memory types have the same speed (registers are fastest, followed by shared memory, then global HBM)

name: NVIDIA GPU Generations for AI
description: Key NVIDIA GPU architectures and their capabilities for AI workloads
key_facts: NVIDIA Ampere (A100) introduced third-generation Tensor Cores with TF32 and structural sparsity support
NVIDIA Hopper (H100) features fourth-generation Tensor Cores with FP8 support and the Transformer Engine
NVLink in Hopper provides up to 900 GB/s bidirectional GPU-to-GPU bandwidth
Multi-Instance GPU (MIG) on A100 and H100 partitions a single GPU into up to 7 isolated instances
NVIDIA Blackwell (B200) is the successor to Hopper, designed for even larger AI model training and inference
common_misconceptions: MIG reduces overall GPU performance (MIG partitions resources but each partition runs at full speed for its allocation)
All NVIDIA data center GPUs support MIG (MIG is only available on A100, H100, and newer architectures)

name: AI Data Center Design
description: Fundamentals of designing data centers for AI workloads including power, cooling, and rack configurations
key_facts: NVIDIA DGX systems are purpose-built servers designed for AI training and inference workloads
GPU-accelerated data centers require significantly higher power density than traditional compute facilities
Liquid cooling is increasingly necessary for high-density GPU deployments to manage thermal output
A DGX H100 system contains 8 H100 GPUs connected via NVLink in a fully connected topology
Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE) measures data center energy efficiency as total facility power divided by IT equipment power
common_misconceptions: Standard air cooling is sufficient for all GPU configurations (high-density GPU racks often require liquid cooling)
DGX systems are just regular servers with GPUs added (they are custom-designed with optimized interconnects, cooling, and software)

name: DGX and HGX Systems Overview
description: NVIDIA DGX and HGX platform capabilities and configurations
key_facts: DGX H100 systems include DGX OS (Ubuntu-based), NVIDIA Base Command, and pre-installed AI software
HGX is a reference GPU computing platform used by OEMs to build their own AI servers
DGX SuperPOD aggregates multiple DGX nodes into a scalable AI training cluster
DGX Cloud provides DGX infrastructure as a managed cloud service through partner cloud providers
Each DGX H100 node delivers approximately 32 petaflops of FP8 AI performance
common_misconceptions: DGX and HGX are the same product (DGX is a complete system; HGX is a reference platform for OEM server builders)
DGX Cloud runs on NVIDIA's own data centers (it is hosted by cloud partners like Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud, and Oracle)

name: AI Networking Fundamentals
description: Basic networking technologies for AI infrastructure including InfiniBand, Ethernet, and NVLink
key_facts: InfiniBand provides low-latency, high-bandwidth interconnect used for GPU-to-GPU communication across nodes
NVIDIA ConnectX SmartNICs provide hardware-accelerated networking for AI data center workloads
NVLink connects GPUs within a node with much higher bandwidth than PCIe
NVSwitch enables all-to-all GPU communication within a DGX system at full NVLink bandwidth
RDMA (Remote Direct Memory Access) allows direct memory access between nodes bypassing the CPU
common_misconceptions: Ethernet is always sufficient for AI training clusters (InfiniBand provides lower latency critical for distributed training)
NVLink and InfiniBand serve the same purpose (NVLink is intra-node GPU-to-GPU; InfiniBand is inter-node)

name: NVLink and NVSwitch
description: High-bandwidth GPU interconnect technologies for intra-node communication
key_facts: NVLink 4.0 in Hopper provides 900 GB/s bidirectional bandwidth per GPU
NVSwitch enables a fully connected topology where every GPU can communicate with every other GPU at full bandwidth
NVLink supports GPU direct memory access, allowing GPUs to read/write each other's memory without CPU involvement
The NVLink domain in a DGX H100 connects all 8 GPUs through NVSwitch for unified memory addressing
NVLink significantly outperforms PCIe Gen5 (128 GB/s) for GPU-to-GPU data transfers
common_misconceptions: NVLink replaces PCIe entirely (PCIe is still used for CPU-to-GPU and I/O device communication)
All GPUs with NVLink have the same bandwidth (bandwidth varies by architecture generation)

name: CUDA and AI Software Stack
description: NVIDIA CUDA platform and AI software ecosystem including drivers, libraries, and frameworks
key_facts: CUDA is NVIDIA's parallel computing platform providing APIs for GPU-accelerated computation
cuDNN is a GPU-accelerated library for deep neural network primitives like convolutions and normalization
The NVIDIA AI software stack layers include drivers, CUDA toolkit, cuDNN/cuBLAS, frameworks, and applications
NVIDIA NGC (NVIDIA GPU Cloud) provides a catalog of pre-built GPU-optimized containers for AI workflows
NCCL (NVIDIA Collective Communications Library) handles multi-GPU and multi-node communication for distributed training
common_misconceptions: CUDA is a programming language (it is a parallel computing platform with extensions for C, C++, and Fortran)
Installing CUDA drivers is sufficient for deep learning (frameworks also need cuDNN and other libraries for optimal performance)

name: NGC Containers and NVIDIA Container Toolkit
description: GPU-accelerated container ecosystem including NGC catalog and container runtime
key_facts: NVIDIA Container Toolkit enables Docker containers to access GPU hardware resources
NGC provides pre-built, tested, and optimized containers for frameworks like PyTorch, TensorFlow, and TensorRT
Container images from NGC include matched versions of CUDA, cuDNN, and framework dependencies
NVIDIA GPU Operator automates GPU driver and toolkit deployment in Kubernetes clusters
Multi-process service (MPS) allows multiple containers to share a single GPU efficiently
common_misconceptions: Standard Docker containers automatically use GPUs (the NVIDIA Container Toolkit runtime is required to expose GPUs)
NGC containers only work on NVIDIA DGX systems (they work on any system with NVIDIA GPUs and the container toolkit)

name: GPU Monitoring and Management
description: Tools and techniques for monitoring GPU health, utilization, and performance
key_facts: nvidia-smi is the primary command-line tool for monitoring GPU status, memory usage, and temperature
DCGM (Data Center GPU Manager) provides comprehensive GPU telemetry, health monitoring, and diagnostics
Key GPU metrics include SM utilization, memory utilization, temperature, power draw, and ECC error counts
DCGM integrates with Prometheus and Grafana for GPU metric visualization in Kubernetes environments
GPU throttling occurs when temperature or power consumption exceeds configured thresholds
common_misconceptions: High GPU utilization always means the workload is optimized (the GPU may be bottlenecked by memory bandwidth or data loading)
nvidia-smi is sufficient for production monitoring (DCGM provides richer telemetry, health checks, and integration with monitoring systems)

name: NVIDIA Base Command and Fleet Management
description: NVIDIA management tools for operating AI infrastructure at scale
key_facts: NVIDIA Base Command Manager provides cluster provisioning, job scheduling, and user management for DGX systems
Base Command Platform is a cloud-hosted service for managing AI training workflows on DGX Cloud
Job scheduling ensures fair sharing of GPU resources across multiple users and teams
Fleet Command enables remote deployment and management of AI applications at edge locations
NVIDIA Run:ai provides GPU orchestration and workload management on Kubernetes clusters
common_misconceptions: Base Command Manager and Base Command Platform are the same product (Manager is on-premises software; Platform is a cloud service)
GPU clusters do not need a job scheduler (without scheduling, GPU resources are easily wasted through poor utilization)
